{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shogun senge]] [[Category:Kamakura Shogunate]] [[Category:Muromachi Shogunate]] [[Category:Edo-period politics]] [[Category:Japanese Samurai Government]] [[Category:Sei-i Taishōgun]] [[Category:Shogunate]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] }} [[sn:Shogun senge]] [[ja:将軍宣下]] [[en:Shogun senge]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shogun senge}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the [[Emperor of Japan]] appoints an individual to the position of [[Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the [[Kamakura period]], shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in [[Kyoto]]. As the [[Kamakura shogunate]] was based in [[Kamakura]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the [[Hōjō clan]], received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[Kenchō]]4th year [[4月1日 (旧暦)|四月一日]] 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[12月25日 (旧暦)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[Senge]] * [[Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

37 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:将軍宣下 up to rev 99625909
revision.text:
'''将軍宣下'''（しょうぐんせんげ）とは、[[天皇]]が[[武家政権]]の長であり[[日本]]の[[統治権|統治大権]]を行使する[[征夷大将軍]]職に任ずる儀式のこと。[[公卿]]（[[三位]]以上）にも[[補任]]されていることから公権力を行使することが制度上認められた。
==概要== [[武家政権]]の長は自己の[[軍事力]]によって[[政権]]を獲得して、[[封建制|封建制度]]的な土地所有と法律による支配を実施した。だが、その政権及びその長としての公認はいまだ中央権力としての地位を保っていた天皇による将軍宣下によって現実的な権力と貴種性の承認によって初めて確立しえた。
もっとも、[[鎌倉時代]]においては原則として将軍宣下は行われていなかったと考えられている。これは当時、[[宣下]]という手続自体が[[京都]]以外に在住した者に対して行われた事例がなく、[[鎌倉]]に住んでいた[[源頼朝]]以下[[鎌倉幕府]]の歴代将軍は[[除目]]によって補任されていたためである。ただし、除目によって任じられる官職は原則的には[[官位相当]]が存在していること<ref group="注釈">鎌倉に在住する者を除目によって征夷大将軍に任じる手続は非在京者に対する補任手続としては正しい（京都に在住する者にしか宣下は行えない）が、征夷大将軍に官位相当が無い以上、その補任手続は[[宣旨]]（宣下）による手続に限定される（官位相当のない征夷大将軍は除目によって任命できない）という矛盾が発生する。</ref>、また[[執権]][[北条氏]]によって京都から新たに迎えられることになった[[宮将軍]]は将軍宣下を受けていることは注目される<ref group="注釈">ただし、[[宗尊親王]]の場合は、突然の事態に手続が下向に間に合わず、親王の鎌倉到着日を4月1日と定め、同日に宣下の事実を[[六波羅探題]]に通告し、後日使者が宣旨を送付する方法が採られた。</ref><ref>北村、[[2005年]]。</ref>。
'''征夷大将軍の辞令（宣旨）の例''' （宗尊親王）（「[[吾妻鏡]]」） 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[建長]]四年[[4月1日 (旧暦)|四月一日]] 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
（訓読文） 三品 宗尊親王 右、左大臣（[[鷹司兼平]]、従一位）の宣を被るに偁（い）はく 件（くだん）の親王、宜しく征夷大将軍に為すべし 建長4年（[[1252年]]）4月1日 大外記中原朝臣師兼（押小路師兼、正五位上）奉（うけたまは）る
'''征夷大将軍の辞令（宣旨案）の例'''（[[足利義晴]]）（「壬生家四巻之日記」） 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大將軍者 [[大永]]元年[[12月25日 (旧暦)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
（訓読文） 左馬頭源朝臣義晴（足利義晴、従五位下） 右中弁藤原朝臣資定（[[柳原資定]]、従四位上）伝へ宣（の）り 権大納言兼大宰権帥藤原朝臣公條（[[三条西公条]]、従二位）宣（の）る 勅（みことのり）を奉（うけたまは）るに、件人（くだんのひと）宜しく征夷大将軍に為すべし者（てへり） [[大永]]元年（[[1521年]]）12月25日 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒（壬生于恒、正五位上）奉（うけたまは）る、
[[近世]]に入ると[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]の権威が失墜して、代わりに[[禁中並公家諸法度]]などによって朝廷にすら支配権を及ぼして「[[公儀]]」の体制と「封建王」的な地位を獲得した[[徳川宗家]]でさえ、その支配の正統性は天皇による将軍宣下に依存しなければならなかった。事実、徳川宗家当主が家督を相続した直後には単に「[[上様]]」と呼ばれ、将軍宣下によって初めて[[清和源氏]]という[[権門]]の長である資格を証明する[[源氏長者]]の地位を公認され、同時に国家的授権行為が行われる事によって「公方様」あるいは「将軍様」となりえた事が示している。そして、実際には「封建王」的存在として朝廷すら支配していた徳川将軍でさえ、将軍宣下と[[上洛]]参内の時には[[天皇]]を「王」、将軍を「[[覇者]]」とする秩序に従っていたのである。
'''征夷大将軍の辞令（宣旨）の例'''（[[徳川家宣]]）（「月堂見聞集」） 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大將軍者 [[宝永|寳永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 修理東大寺大佛長官主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
（訓読文） 権大納言源朝臣家宣（徳川家宣、正二位） 右中弁兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光（裏松益光、正五位上）伝へ宣（の）り 権大納言藤原朝臣基勝（園基勝、従二位）宣（の）る 勅（みことのり）を奉（うけたまは）るに、件人（くだんのひと）宜しく征夷大将軍に為すべし者（てへり） 宝永6年（[[1709年]]）4月2日 修理東大寺大仏長官主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘（壬生章弘、正五位上）奉（うけたまは）る、
将軍宣下の際は、[[江戸時代]]の大半を通じて、[[江戸城]]に[[勅使]]が赴き、将軍が上座、勅使が下座に立って宣下を行ったが、[[幕末]]期には[[皇室]]・[[公家]]の権威が[[尊王論|尊王思想]]の影響で回復を遂げたため、[[徳川家茂]]以降、勅使が上座、将軍が下座となった。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *吉田昌彦「将軍宣下」『歴史学事典 12<small>王と国家</small>』 [[弘文堂]]、[[2005年]] ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3 *北村拓「鎌倉幕府征夷大将軍の補任について」（所収:今江廣道 編『中世の史料と制度』（続群書類従完成会、[[2005年]]） ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2 P137-194）
==関連項目== *[[宣下]] *[[征夷大将軍]] *[[大政委任論]] *[[大政奉還]] *[[武家の棟梁]] *[[天下人]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[将軍宣下]] to [[Shogun senge]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''将軍宣下'''（しょうぐんせんげ）とは、[[天皇]]が[[武家政権]]の長であり[[日本]]の[[統治権|統治大権]]を行使する[[征夷大将軍]]職に任ずる儀式のこと。[[公卿]]（[[三位]]以上）にも[[補任]]されていることから公権力を行使することが制度上認められた。
==概要== [[武家政権]]の長は自己の[[軍事力]]によって[[政権]]を獲得して、[[封建制|封建制度]]的な土地所有と法律による支配を実施した。だが、その政権及びその長としての公認はいまだ中央権力としての地位を保っていた天皇による将軍宣下によって現実的な権力と貴種性の承認によって初めて確立しえた。
もっとも、[[鎌倉時代]]においては原則として将軍宣下は行われていなかったと考えられている。これは当時、[[宣下]]という手続自体が[[京都]]以外に在住した者に対して行われた事例がなく、[[鎌倉]]に住んでいた[[源頼朝]]以下[[鎌倉幕府]]の歴代将軍は[[除目]]によって補任されていたためである。ただし、除目によって任じられる官職は原則的には[[官位相当]]が存在していること<ref group="注釈">鎌倉に在住する者を除目によって征夷大将軍に任じる手続は非在京者に対する補任手続としては正しい（京都に在住する者にしか宣下は行えない）が、征夷大将軍に官位相当が無い以上、その補任手続は[[宣旨]]（宣下）による手続に限定される（官位相当のない征夷大将軍は除目によって任命できない）という矛盾が発生する。</ref>、また[[執権]][[北条氏]]によって京都から新たに迎えられることになった[[宮将軍]]は将軍宣下を受けていることは注目される<ref group="注釈">ただし、[[宗尊親王]]の場合は、突然の事態に手続が下向に間に合わず、親王の鎌倉到着日を4月1日と定め、同日に宣下の事実を[[六波羅探題]]に通告し、後日使者が宣旨を送付する方法が採られた。</ref><ref>北村、[[2005年]]。</ref>。
'''征夷大将軍の辞令（宣旨）の例''' （宗尊親王）（「[[吾妻鏡]]」） 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[建長]]四年[[4月1日 (旧暦)|四月一日]] 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
（訓読文） 三品 宗尊親王 右、左大臣（[[鷹司兼平]]、従一位）の宣を被るに偁（い）はく 件（くだん）の親王、宜しく征夷大将軍に為すべし 建長4年（[[1252年]]）4月1日 大外記中原朝臣師兼（押小路師兼、正五位上）奉（うけたまは）る
'''征夷大将軍の辞令（宣旨案）の例'''（[[足利義晴]]）（「壬生家四巻之日記」） 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大將軍者 [[大永]]元年[[12月25日 (旧暦)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
（訓読文） 左馬頭源朝臣義晴（足利義晴、従五位下） 右中弁藤原朝臣資定（[[柳原資定]]、従四位上）伝へ宣（の）り 権大納言兼大宰権帥藤原朝臣公條（[[三条西公条]]、従二位）宣（の）る 勅（みことのり）を奉（うけたまは）るに、件人（くだんのひと）宜しく征夷大将軍に為すべし者（てへり） [[大永]]元年（[[1521年]]）12月25日 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒（壬生于恒、正五位上）奉（うけたまは）る、
[[近世]]に入ると[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]の権威が失墜して、代わりに[[禁中並公家諸法度]]などによって朝廷にすら支配権を及ぼして「[[公儀]]」の体制と「封建王」的な地位を獲得した[[徳川宗家]]でさえ、その支配の正統性は天皇による将軍宣下に依存しなければならなかった。事実、徳川宗家当主が家督を相続した直後には単に「[[上様]]」と呼ばれ、将軍宣下によって初めて[[清和源氏]]という[[権門]]の長である資格を証明する[[源氏長者]]の地位を公認され、同時に国家的授権行為が行われる事によって「公方様」あるいは「将軍様」となりえた事が示している。そして、実際には「封建王」的存在として朝廷すら支配していた徳川将軍でさえ、将軍宣下と[[上洛]]参内の時には[[天皇]]を「王」、将軍を「[[覇者]]」とする秩序に従っていたのである。
'''征夷大将軍の辞令（宣旨）の例'''（[[徳川家宣]]）（「月堂見聞集」） 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大將軍者 [[宝永|寳永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 修理東大寺大佛長官主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
（訓読文） 権大納言源朝臣家宣（徳川家宣、正二位） 右中弁兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光（裏松益光、正五位上）伝へ宣（の）り 権大納言藤原朝臣基勝（園基勝、従二位）宣（の）る 勅（みことのり）を奉（うけたまは）るに、件人（くだんのひと）宜しく征夷大将軍に為すべし者（てへり） 宝永6年（[[1709年]]）4月2日 修理東大寺大仏長官主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘（壬生章弘、正五位上）奉（うけたまは）る、
将軍宣下の際は、[[江戸時代]]の大半を通じて、[[江戸城]]に[[勅使]]が赴き、将軍が上座、勅使が下座に立って宣下を行ったが、[[幕末]]期には[[皇室]]・[[公家]]の権威が[[尊王論|尊王思想]]の影響で回復を遂げたため、[[徳川家茂]]以降、勅使が上座、将軍が下座となった。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *吉田昌彦「将軍宣下」『歴史学事典 12<small>王と国家</small>』 [[弘文堂]]、[[2005年]] ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3 *北村拓「鎌倉幕府征夷大将軍の補任について」（所収:今江廣道 編『中世の史料と制度』（続群書類従完成会、[[2005年]]） ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2 P137-194）
==関連項目== *[[宣下]] *[[征夷大将軍]] *[[大政委任論]] *[[大政奉還]] *[[武家の棟梁]] *[[天下人]]

revision.text:
'''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the [[Emperor of Japan]] appoints an individual to the position of [[Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the [[Kamakura period]], shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in [[Kyoto]]. As the [[Kamakura shogunate]] was based in [[Kamakura]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the [[Hōjō clan]], received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[Kenchō]]4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[Senge]] * [[Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11458629]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] appoints an individual to the position of [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[:en:feudal system|feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. As the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was based in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]]4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[:en:Senge|Senge]] * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}

=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[:en:Senge|Senge]] * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458629]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] appoints an individual to the position of [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[:en:feudal system|feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. As the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was based in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]]4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[:en:Senge|Senge]] * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]

(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458629]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] appoints an individual to the position of [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[:en:feudal system|feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. As the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was based in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]]4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉

As the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was based in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]]4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[:en:Senge|Senge]] * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458629]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] appoints an individual to the position of [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[:en:feudal system|feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. As the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was based in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]]4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉

== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[:en:Senge|Senge]] * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458629]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] appoints an individual to the position of [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[:en:feudal system|feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. As the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was based in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]]4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[:en:Senge|Senge]] * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|将軍宣下|version=99625909|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵↵" to " "
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458629]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] appoints an individual to the position of [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[:en:feudal system|feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. As the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was based in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]]4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[:en:Senge|Senge]] * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|将軍宣下|version=99625909|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}

revision.comment: Bot: reorganize categories (alphabetical, deduplicated, moved to bottom)
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458629]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] appoints an individual to the position of [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[:en:feudal system|feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. As the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was based in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]]4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[:en:Senge|Senge]] * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{draft categories|

=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉

"On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. {{wikidata link|Q11458629}} [[Category:Ashikaga shogunate]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Government of feudal Japan]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Politics of Japan in the Edo period]] [[Category:Shogunates of Japan]] [[Category:Shōguns]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵↵" to " "
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458629]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] appoints an individual to the position of [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[:en:feudal system|feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. As the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was based in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]]4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[:en:Senge|Senge]] * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[:en:Senge|Senge]] * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458629]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] appoints an individual to the position of [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[:en:feudal system|feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. As the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was based in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]]4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiharu|Ashikaga Yoshiharu]]: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 [[:en:Daiei (era)|Daiei]]元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of [[:en:Tokugawa Ienobu|Tokugawa Ienobu]]: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * [[:en:Senge|Senge]] * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]
