However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. As the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}} was based in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura}}, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}}'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō}}4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiharu|en|Ashikaga Yoshiharu|WD=Q536313|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiharu}}: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 {{ill|Daiei (era)|en|Daiei (era)|WD=Q1193564|lt=Daiei}}元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}} lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of {{ill|Tokugawa Ienobu|en|Tokugawa Ienobu|WD=Q366734|lt=Tokugawa Ienobu}}: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * {{ill|Senge|en|Senge|WD=Q17107807|lt=Senge}} * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458629]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}} appoints an individual to the position of [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[:en:feudal system|feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. As the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}} was based in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura}}, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}}'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō}}4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiharu|en|Ashikaga Yoshiharu|WD=Q536313|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiharu}}: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 {{ill|Daiei (era)|en|Daiei (era)|WD=Q1193564|lt=Daiei}}元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}} lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of {{ill|Tokugawa Ienobu|en|Tokugawa Ienobu|WD=Q366734|lt=Tokugawa Ienobu}}: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉

"On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * {{ill|Senge|en|Senge|WD=Q17107807|lt=Senge}} * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]]
'''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}} appoints an individual to the position of [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[:en:feudal system|feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. As the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}} was based in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura}}, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}}'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō}}4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiharu|en|Ashikaga Yoshiharu|WD=Q536313|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiharu}}: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 {{ill|Daiei (era)|en|Daiei (era)|WD=Q1193564|lt=Daiei}}元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}} lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of {{ill|Tokugawa Ienobu|en|Tokugawa Ienobu|WD=Q366734|lt=Tokugawa Ienobu}}: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * {{ill|Senge|en|Senge|WD=Q17107807|lt=Senge}} * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of {{ill|Tokugawa Ienobu|en|Tokugawa Ienobu|WD=Q366734|lt=Tokugawa Ienobu|1=Tokugawa Ienobu}}: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * {{ill|Senge|en|Senge|WD=Q17107807|lt=Senge|1=Senge}} * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:将軍宣下]][[ko:장군선하]]
'''Shōgun Senge''' (将軍宣下) refers to the formal ceremony in which the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} appoints an individual to the position of [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] (征夷大将軍), granting them the authority to act as the head of the [[samurai government]] and exercise governance over Japan. This ceremony also served as a formal acknowledgment of the individual's legitimacy as a wielder of public authority, which was recognized by being conferred official Rank and title within the imperial court system.
== Overview == The leaders of samurai governments often gained power through their military strength and established rule based on a [[:en:feudal system|feudal system]] of land ownership and law enforcement. However, their recognition as legitimate rulers and their claim to noble lineage were only fully established through the shōgun senge, a process officiated by the emperor, who retained a symbolic central authority.
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, shōgun senge was not typically conducted. This was because the procedure of ''senge'' (imperial proclamation) was only performed for individuals residing in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. As the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}} was based in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and subsequent shoguns were appointed through ''jomei'' (a court procedure for granting official titles). However, ''jomei'' typically required the position to have a corresponding Rank within the court hierarchy.<ref group="note">The appointment of a Sei-i Taishōgun, who lacked a defined Rank, through ''jomei'' procedures created a contradiction, as such positions were generally designated through ''senji'' (imperial proclamation).</ref> Notably, shoguns of imperial lineage, such as those of the [[Miyashogun]] (imperial shoguns) appointed by the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan|1=Hōjō clan}}, received shōgun senge.<ref group="note">In the case of [[Prince Munetaka]], the proclamation was sent to Kamakura after his arrival due to delays in the formal process.</ref><ref>Kitamura, 2005.</ref>
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === Prince Munetaka's appointment as recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}}'': 三品 [[宗尊親王]] 右被左大臣宣偁件親王宜征夷大将軍 {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō|1=Kenchō}}4th year {{ill|April 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月1日 (旧暦)|lt=四月一日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} 大外記中原朝臣師兼奉
(Translation): Prince Munetaka, of Third Rank, Having received the declaration of the Minister of the Left (Takaishi Kanehira, Senior First Rank): "Let this prince be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 4th year of Kenchō (1252), 4th month, 1st day. Written by the Senior Official, Nakanohara Morikane, Fifth Rank Upper.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation Draft === The draft for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiharu|en|Ashikaga Yoshiharu|WD=Q536313|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiharu|1=Ashikaga Yoshiharu}}: 左馬頭源朝臣義晴 右中辨藤原朝臣資定傳宣 權大納言兼大宰權帥藤原朝臣公條宣 奉 勅件人宜為征夷大将軍者 {{ill|Daiei (era)|en|Daiei (era)|WD=Q1193564|lt=Daiei|1=Daiei (era)}}元年[[December 25 (Lunisolar)|十二月廿五日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰于恒奉
(Translation): Saemon-no-Kami Minamoto no Yoshiharu (Ashikaga Yoshiharu, Junior Fifth Rank Lower), Received the declaration of the Vice-Minister of the Right, Fujiwara no Sukeyuki (Junior Fourth Rank Upper), And the declaration of Acting Grand Counselor, Fujiwara no Kinjō (Junior Second Rank): "It is decreed that the aforementioned individual be appointed Sei-i Taishōgun." 25th day, 12th month, 1st year of Daiei (1521). Written by Chief of Repairs for the Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji Temple, Junior Fifth Rank Upper, Mibu Sadafusa.
== Shōgun Senge in Later Periods == In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, even the powerful [[Tokugawa clan]], which held de facto control over the imperial court through laws like the [[Buke Shohatto]], depended on shōgun senge to legitimize their rule. The head of the Tokugawa family, upon inheriting leadership, was initially referred to as "Lord" (上様). Only after the shōgun senge was performed did they gain recognition as the leader of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} lineage and assume the titles "Kubo-sama" or "Shōgun-sama," with the public affirmation of their authority.
Interestingly, while the Tokugawa shoguns exercised authority akin to that of a "feudal king," they adhered to the ceremonial order in which the emperor was recognized as "king" and the shogun as "hegemony" during the shōgun senge and formal court visits.
=== Example of Sei-i Taishōgun Proclamation === The appointment of {{ill|Tokugawa Ienobu|en|Tokugawa Ienobu|WD=Q366734|lt=Tokugawa Ienobu|1=Tokugawa Ienobu}}: 權大納言源朝臣家宣 右中辨兼春宮大進藤原朝臣益光傳宣 權大納言藤原朝臣基勝宣 奉 勅件人宜爲征夷大将軍者 [[:en:宝永|宝永]]六年[[4月2日 (旧暦)|四月二日]] 主殿頭兼左大史小槻宿禰章弘奉
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Yoshida, Masahiko. "Shōgun Senge," in ''Historical Dictionary 12: Kings and States''. Kōbundō, 2005. ISBN 978-4-335-21043-3. * Kitamura, Taku. "On the Appointment of Kamakura Shogunate Sei-i Taishōgun," in Imae, Hiromichi (ed.), ''Medieval Sources and Systems''. Zoku Gunsho Ruijū, 2005. ISBN 978-4-7971-0743-2. == See Also == * {{ill|Senge|en|Senge|WD=Q17107807|lt=Senge|1=Senge}} * [[:en:Sei-i Taishōgun|Sei-i Taishōgun]] * [[Taisei-inin-ron]] * [[Taisei-hōkan]] * [[Samurai Leadership]] * [[Tenkajin]]
